content:
- Pluxee Check-in with Ramki – Office Hours Structured Summary

- II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call

- This monthly Pluxee check-in was led by Ramki Gaddipati for comprehensive status updates across squads focusing on Pluxee India and Philippines projects. The conversation traversed through live deliverables, persistent challenges (notably in UAT and production bugs), technical debt initiatives (mainly legacy refactoring), and operational health parameters (SLAs, incident management, hiring trends). Ramki actively challenged the team’s assumptions on delivery timelines, definitions of "done," and insisted on sharper metrics and responsibility boundaries. The dialogue included robust discussions on rollouts (such as SSO migration and Infibeam recharge), performance monitoring, and approaches to automation and refactoring, including leveraging modern tooling. Operational stability and process hygiene were recurrent touchpoints, with a strong emphasis from Ramki on cross-functional visibility, error budgeting, and next-step clarity. Towards closure, the team reflected on a visible positive trajectory in operational health and client satisfaction.

- IV. Key Takeaways from the Call

- Live Features Nearing Completion: Multiple deliverables like SSO migration and Infibeam recharge are in final rollout/bug-fixing stages, with teams targeting end-of-May and June deadlines ("doing all the right things to ensure...").
- Definitions of Done & Bug Handling Clarified: Ramki reinforced that once features are released to UAT, any issues must be seen as production bugs and tracked with production SLAs, cementing accountability ("You can't have both sides...").
- Legacy Component Migration Drive: Aggressive timeline set for migrating/removing legacy mobile components and OMS modules, with June 2025 as a major milestone.
- Modern Tooling Encouraged for Refactoring: Ramki promoted prompt-based code refactoring (e.g., using AI/dev tools for OMS to Spring Boot transformation) as a hypothesis for internal evaluation.
- Performance Center and Observability Emphasized: Consolidation and surfacing of transaction/business KPIs and transition to a unified report center remain cornerstones, with tangible deadlines set.
- SRE, Incident, and SLA Fortification: Incident SLAs, error budgeting, and tight triage practices are in place; Ramki wants parity between Sev-level bugs and standard bug SLAs, with added cadence for aligning dev/SRE on priorities.
- Operational Health & Client Service Trends: QRC and ticket SLAs have markedly improved, with lower ticket volumes and positive client satisfaction signals ("a lot more peace and a sense of confidence").
- Code Jam Scheduling & Participation: Early June code jam slated; Ramki wants broad team involvement (engineering, product, delivery) to foster shared understanding and connection to core technical work.

- V. Ramki's Insights

1. On Philippines Project Delivery Pace:
- Ramki expressed skepticism about the slow pace of delivery and reiterates the need to examine not only the team’s viewpoint but also to solicit the client's perspective directly. He urges not to assume internal interpretations are sufficient and recognizes the customer's hesitancy and caution as indicative of trust challenges. His tone here is lightly probing and facilitative, aimed at balancing perspectives rather than assigning blame.

2. Definition of Done & Bug Lifecycle:
- With clarity and insistence, Ramki established that any release into UAT, if deemed "delivered," must be tracked with production-level rigor in case of defects; thus, teams must not shift responsibility away from the post-release stage. His approach was assertive and uncompromising, driving home a standardized accountability metric.

3. Challenging Timelines & Driving Urgency:
- Ramki routinely pushed back on teams for extended deadlines (e.g., asking "Why is the date... so far away?"), urging teams to accelerate, reevaluate, and avoid padding timelines needlessly. He set the expectation for earlier deliverables and refused to accept defensive reasoning without substantiation. This was delivered with a direct, impatient tone emphasizing result orientation.

4. Modernization via Tooling and Prompts:
- He advocated for trialing AI/devops tools to automate code migration (OMS to Spring Boot), shaping this as an experiment/hypothesis to test within the squad. He seeks demonstrable efficiency from modern tools, recommending an iterative, hackathon-like exploration. His tone is innovative, pragmatic, and open to "fail fast" learning.

5. Incident and SLA Philosophy:
- Ramki mapped incident priorities to defect management protocols, demanding equivalent SLA treatment for P1/P2 problems and bugs. He articulated that SRE and engineering teams must regularly calibrate priorities and align on problem ticket handling, showing process discipline and cross-team alignment as critical.

6. Reflection on Team Progress and Client Perception:
- Ramki paused to acknowledge visible improvements in orderliness, operational health, and client ticket handling. He cautioned, however, that internal progress should translate to tangible effectiveness as perceived by clients, advocating for continuous improvement in customer CSAT and not resting on internal assessments alone. His closing remarks were supportive, motivational, and future-focused on sustaining the operational trajectory.

- VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items

1. Legacy OMS Refactor Using Modern Tooling
- Task: Explore the use of development prompts/AI tools to refactor legacy OMS codebases to Spring Boot; develop/refine prompts, pilot with exemplar modules, and assess feasibility.
- Assigned To: Ashish, Subbu, Virender (with Balaji/Kirit to coordinate)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Run as an internal hackathon/targeted experiment; encourage fine-tuning of prompts, "try those prompts," and evaluate if this technologically modern approach accelerates migration. Aim for hands-on, outcome-focused pilots.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: No fixed date, but urgency in kickstarting and reporting outcomes soon.

2. Accelerate Key Deliverable Timelines (e.g., Tap & Pay Issue, SSO Rollout)
- Task: Shorten planned delivery timelines for in-progress features (notably tap & pay fixes and SSO rollout), aiming for earliest possible completion.
- Assigned To: Relevant engineering leads (e.g., Balaji, Hari, team leads)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Avoid excessive schedule padding, question timeline assumptions, escalate blockers, and move up timing wherever feasible. Treat as primary focus.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Move scheduled completions earlier in June, specifically for code jam and feature finalization.

3. SRE/Incident Priority Alignment and Process Adherence
- Task: Ensure problem tickets (P1/P2) are managed and tracked with equivalent SLA as defects; set up regular cadence for triage and resolution agreement between SRE and dev teams.
- Assigned To: Akhil (SRE), Ashish, Subbu, SRE/dev leads
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Reiterate process discipline, "same as P1 bug," create explicit agreement meetings, and enforce tracking through closure. Maintain error budgeting as separate but not as a substitute for SLA discipline.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Immediate and ongoing.

4. Ensure Broad Engagement in Code Jam
- Task: Finalize early June code jam and ensure wide participation from all relevant functions (engineering, product, delivery, non-coders).
- Assigned To: Balaji (owner), Vinay, entire leadership team
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Schedule for early June (not later), push for participatory discussion where everyone can follow the conversation, not just coders, to foster cross-functional insight.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Early June 2025

- VII. Jira Ticket

Scope:
- "OMS Code Migration to Spring Boot" – scope should encompass pilot module refactoring using AI/developer prompts, capturing the methodology and outcomes to inform broader migration.
- Accelerating timelines for feature deliveries (SSO, tap & pay) are continuous operational track items and may not fit a finite Jira unless tied to specific, well-scoped engineering tasks.
- SRE/Incident SLA harmonization is primarily a process/cadence action and not directly suited to Jira; implementation of process agreements/cadence could be tracked in confluence or process tools rather than Jira.

Assignee:
- OMS Modernization: Ashish, Subbu, Virender, (Balaji as coordinator)
- No new Jira ticket recommended for process/SLA changes or general code jam participation.

Justification:
- OMS refactoring is a concrete, trackable engineering initiative that stands to benefit from structured reporting and iterative demonstration in Jira.
- Other action items are better captured as meetings, process calendar entries, or operational objectives due to their continuous/process or event-driven nature.

- VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions

- When trialing prompt-based OMS refactoring, document not only code transformation outcomes but also developer experience, accuracy, and any blockers encountered—this will clarify broader feasibility and inform organizational adoption.
- For UAT/production bug process, periodically review release/bounce-back instances to identify any ambiguities or disputes in bug categorization or SLA assignment, enabling continual process refinement.
- For recurring incidents/SLA monitoring, consider scheduling regular joint review sessions between SRE and dev leadership to calibrate priorities and transparency on blockers, ensuring alignment remains active and issues do not regress.
- As code jam is scheduled, solicit specific pain points or improvement ideas from both technical and non-technical participants in advance, to ensure sessions address both known and latent challenges.
- With improvements in QRC SLAs and CSAT, consider publishing these outcomes in client communications to proactively reinforce operational progress and trust, closing perception gaps.
title: 2025-05-19_Pluxee Check-in with Ramki - Monthly (5)
